extra.reasoning: This is a state machine. The property requires globally(opted_out implies not sent): no reachable state may have opted_out=true AND sent=true. Transition [t5] (on send_attempt if opted_out and ticks>=2: sent:=true) directly sets sent true while opted_out is true, so it is the suspect rule. I constructed a reachable execution within the 6-event bound: (1) optout -> via t1: opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=0; (2) tick -> via t4: ticks=1, sent=false; (3) tick -> via t4: ticks=2, sent=false; (4) send_attempt -> now opted_out=true and ticks>=2, so t5 is enabled and fires, sent:=true, yielding state opted_out=true, sent=true. This state violates opted_out implies not sent. Note that at step 4 both t3 (guard opted_out -> sent:=false) and t5 (guard opted_out and ticks>=2 -> sent:=true) are enabled; since t5 is a defined enabled transition that reaches a violating state, the property does not hold on every reachable execution. The plain-language intent (an opted-out member never has sent=true) is contradicted by rule t5, which re-permits sending once two ticks have elapsed after opt-out. Counterexample uses 4 events, within the bound.
